title: US Closes AI Chip Loophole, DeepSeek Raises $7B, China Plans Compute Futures
subhead: The US Commerce Department moved to block advanced chip exports to Chinese subsidiaries abroad, while DeepSeek secured its first external funding and Shanghai announced plans for compute futures—three developments that signal intensifying competition over AI infrastructure and capital.

sections.heading: US Closes Year-Long Loophole on AI Chip Exports to Chinese Subsidiaries
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department issued surprise guidance on Sunday requiring licenses for advanced AI chip exports to entities headquartered in China—even when those entities are located outside China. The move closes a potential loophole that may have allowed hundreds of thousands of Nvidia's most sophisticated Rubin and Blackwell processors, as well as AMD's MI350x chips, to reach Chinese AI firms through subsidiaries in places like Malaysia over the past year, according to Reuters via SCMP.

The guidance suggests US chips were making their way to Chinese entities despite broader export controls designed to starve Chinese firms of semiconductors needed for advanced AI development. One chip industry source with deep supply-chain knowledge estimated the number of chips exported during the loophole period in the hundreds of thousands.
sections.whyItMatters: This late-Sunday guidance reveals how seriously Washington treats AI compute leakage—and how difficult enforcement remains. The fact that a year-long gap existed underscores the challenge of controlling intangible technology flows across global supply chains.

sections.heading: DeepSeek Near $7 Billion First Funding Round at $60 Billion Valuation
sections.body:
Chinese AI startup DeepSeek is finalizing its first external fundraising round, securing over 50 billion yuan ($7.4 billion) at a valuation just under $60 billion—a six-fold increase from its $10 billion valuation in April, according to SCMP. Founder and CEO Liang Wenfeng is committing around 20 billion yuan of his own capital.

Tencent Holdings is expected to invest 10 billion yuan, while NetEase and JD.com will each contribute about 3 billion yuan. CATL, the world's largest EV battery maker, is expected to invest around 5 billion yuan. Venture capital firms including IDG Capital, Monolith, Loyal Valley Capital, and Shixiang Tech are also participating.

The blockbuster round highlights intensifying global competition and a shifting strategy for DeepSeek, which had previously resisted external capital. The company has emerged as China's most prominent AI challenger to US models like OpenAI's GPT series.
sections.whyItMatters: DeepSeek's rapid valuation jump—from $10B to nearly $60B in two months—reflects investor appetite for Chinese AI infrastructure plays even as export controls tighten. The participation of Tencent, JD.com, and CATL signals major Chinese tech and industrial players are rallying around domestic AI capability.

sections.heading: China Plans Compute Futures as AI Infrastructure Becomes Financial Asset
sections.body:
Shanghai's municipal government announced plans to launch compute futures contracts, making the city the first in China to explicitly include computing power derivatives in official financial planning. The announcement comes just three weeks after Chicago-based CME Group said it would launch the world's first compute futures this year.

According to SCMP, the Shanghai guidelines state the new financial derivative would form part of efforts to turn the megacity into a global wealth management hub. Shanghai is home to China's largest commodity futures bourse, the Shanghai Futures Exchange.

The contracts would allow traders, financial institutions, AI developers, and cloud service providers to hedge against volatility and pricing risks in computing power—as central to the AI boom as electricity was to industrialization.
sections.whyItMatters: Compute is becoming a tradeable commodity. Shanghai's move signals Beijing's intent to build financial infrastructure around AI computing capacity, creating hedging instruments for companies facing volatile demand and limited supply. The race with CME to establish compute futures markets reflects competition over who sets pricing benchmarks for AI infrastructure.

sections.heading: Hong Kong Launches DeepSeek-Based Model Running on Domestic Chips
sections.body:
The Hong Kong Generative AI Research and Development Centre (HKGAI) officially launched HKGAI-V3, a DeepSeek-based large language model designed to run on domestic Chinese chips, according to SCMP. The government-backed lab said the model achieved 'significant improvements' in efficiency and agentic capabilities, including over tenfold improvement in token compression efficiency.

The Agent Workshop platform, developed based on HKGAI-V3, operated stably for up to 28 hours without interruption in a single session to produce a research report, according to HKGAI director Guo Yike. The centre, established in October 2023 under Hong Kong's InnoHK funding program, focuses on AI models tailored to Hong Kong's needs and operates HKChat, the city's first Cantonese-enabled government chatbot.
sections.whyItMatters: Hong Kong is positioning itself as a launch pad for mainland AI firms seeking global markets while navigating export controls. The ability to run advanced models on domestic chips addresses a critical vulnerability for Chinese AI development as US restrictions tighten on advanced semiconductors.

sections.heading: Mainland Chinese Delegates Locked Out of Taiwan's Computex Show
sections.body:
As Computex Taipei—one of the world's largest tech trade shows—convened this week under the theme 'AI Together,' mainland Chinese exhibitors found themselves unable to secure entry permits from Taiwanese authorities, according to SCMP. Two travel agencies specializing in cross-strait travel said none of their clients received entry-permit approvals this year.

The show features over 1,500 exhibitors from 33 countries, including US giants Nvidia, Intel, and AMD. But mainland Chinese residents working for exhibitors reported applications left pending indefinitely or asked for documentation nearly impossible to provide on short notice.

A similar 'visa freeze' occurred in April at the Taipei AMPA automobile parts show. The effective exclusion comes amid heightened cross-strait tensions and reflects Taiwan's growing caution about Chinese access to its semiconductor ecosystem.
sections.whyItMatters: Taiwan's de facto exclusion of mainland Chinese from its premier tech showcase underscores how geopolitical tensions are fragmenting the global technology community. Even as Taiwan remains central to global chip supply, the island is restricting Chinese access to technology showcases and networking opportunities.

sections.heading: Tencent, Alibaba Race to Embed AI Agents in China's Digital Superhighways
sections.body:
Tencent shares surged 10% on Tuesday after the Financial Times reported the company is close to launching an AI agent within WeChat, the super app with 1.4 billion active users. According to SCMP, Tencent is testing a prototype and plans to start compliance procedures for a public launch as soon as this month.

Hours later, Alibaba announced it was opening its Qwen AI assistant to major brands, allowing users to order food, book flights, and make purchases through natural conversation. KFC, Luckin Coffee, Mixue Group, and China Eastern Airlines are among the first partners testing agentic features on the platform, which now facilitates over 100 million daily engagements for lifestyle services.

The dueling announcements signal intensifying competition to become China's primary AI gateway—leveraging existing ecosystems of payments, mini-programs, and commerce infrastructure to embed AI assistants into daily digital life.
sections.whyItMatters: The battle for China's AI gateway is accelerating. Tencent's WeChat and Alibaba's ecosystem together cover most Chinese digital life; whoever embeds AI agents successfully will shape how 1.4 billion users interact with services, information, and commerce. This is infrastructure-level competition.

sections.heading: Memory Chip Price Surge Squeezes Chinese Smartphone Makers
sections.body:
Chinese smartphone makers are preparing for their most challenging year since the pandemic as skyrocketing memory chip prices force brands to absorb higher costs, raise retail prices, or downgrade storage configurations, according to Counterpoint research cited by SCMP.

Global smartphone shipments are projected to plunge nearly 14% this year to about 1.08 billion units—the industry's lowest volume since 2013. Apple and Samsung are expected to weather the downturn best, shielded by stronger pricing power and premium portfolios.

Chinese Android vendors including Xiaomi, Oppo, Vivo, and Transsion face harsher profit pressure due to thinner margins and heavier reliance on price-sensitive segments. Huawei is projected to be the only domestic Chinese brand to increase shipments despite the memory squeeze, according to Counterpoint.
sections.whyItMatters: Memory chip price volatility is testing the business models of Chinese handset makers already squeezed by geopolitical headwinds. Huawei's relative resilience reflects its domestic supply chain depth—but for Xiaomi and others, component costs may accelerate consolidation or force market share concessions.

bottomLine: Today's developments crystallize the three-front competition over AI infrastructure: the US tightening export controls to close loopholes, Chinese firms raising capital and building domestic alternatives, and financial markets beginning to trade compute as a commodity. DeepSeek's $60 billion valuation and Shanghai's compute futures announcement signal Beijing's intent to build out AI infrastructure as a strategic asset class—while Washington's Sunday guidance shows how seriously it treats leakage through subsidiary networks. The exclusion of mainland Chinese from Computex adds another data point to the fragmenting of global tech communities along geopolitical lines. Meanwhile, Tencent and Alibaba's race to embed AI agents into super apps reveals where the consumer-facing battle will be fought: not in labs, but in the 1.4 billion-user platforms that shape daily digital life in China.
sources: South China Morning Post, Reuters, The Verge, Defense News, Financial Times, Counterpoint Research
